Abandoned Cart Email Recovery Sequence

Generate a 3-email abandoned cart recovery sequence with escalating urgency — a gentle reminder, a value reinforcement with social proof, and a final offer with scarcity. Each email includes subject line, preview text, body copy, and CTA.

Intermediate STEP BY STEP Revenue-driving
Pro tip

The first email should go out within 1 hour of abandonment — this single timing change recovers more carts than any copy improvement. The second email at 24 hours. The third at 48-72 hours with a small incentive. Never lead with a discount — you train customers to abandon carts on purpose.

abandoned cart email sequence ecommerce recovery conversion email marketing

Prompt Variants by Model

Claude Claude 4.x
FRESH APR 2026 
You are an e-commerce email strategist. Design an abandoned cart recovery sequence. Think through the psychology of each email step by step.

<store_context>
Business: [NAME] — [WHAT YOU...

<store_context>
Business: [NAME] — [WHAT YOU...

Unlock this prompt + all 74 in the library — free, updated monthly, zero spam.

Your email unlocks all 74 prompts instantly. No password, no signup — just your email. 

You are an e-commerce email strategist. Design an abandoned cart recovery sequence. Think through the psychology of each email step by step.

<store_context>
Business: [NAME] — [WHAT YOU SELL]
Average cart value: $[AMOUNT]
Product type: [CONSIDERED PURCHASE / IMPULSE BUY / SUBSCRIPTION]
Brand tone: [e.g., "playful and casual", "premium and restrained", "helpful and warm"]
Can you offer a discount: [YES — max % / NO — use other incentives]
Social proof available: [REVIEW COUNT, RATING, TESTIMONIALS, PRESS MENTIONS]
</store_context>

Think through the buyer psychology at each stage:
- 1 hour after abandonment: what is the buyer feeling? (distracted, forgot, price hesitation, comparison shopping)
- 24 hours: what has changed? (they may have bought elsewhere, or the desire has faded)
- 48-72 hours: last chance psychology — what motivates action now?

Then generate:

**Email 1 — The Gentle Reminder (1 hour):**
- Subject line (under 50 chars, NO discount mentioned)
- Preview text (under 90 chars)
- Body: friendly nudge, show the product, remove friction ("still in your cart"), link back to cart
- CTA: "Complete your order" (not "Buy now" — too aggressive for email 1)
- Tone: helpful, not salesy

**Email 2 — Value Reinforcement (24 hours):**
- Subject line (curiosity or social proof angle)
- Preview text
- Body: reinforce WHY this product is worth it. Include social proof (review quote, star rating, number of customers). Address the most common objection for this product type.
- CTA: "See why [X] customers love this"

**Email 3 — Final Offer (48-72 hours):**
- Subject line (urgency or incentive)
- Preview text
- Body: create scarcity (limited stock, cart expiring, or small discount). If discount: frame as exclusive, not desperation. If no discount: offer free shipping, bonus item, or extended guarantee.
- CTA: "Claim your [incentive] before it expires"

For each email: note the send timing, expected open rate benchmark, and what to do if they convert mid-sequence (suppress remaining emails).
Notes: The chain-of-thought psychology step prevents generic "you forgot something" emails. Claude builds the escalation arc intentionally.

Your email unlocks all 74 prompts instantly. No password, no signup — just your email. 

Act as an e-commerce email strategist. Build an abandoned cart recovery sequence.

**Store:**
- Business: [NAME] — [WHAT YOU SELL]
- Average cart: $[AMOUNT]
- Product type: [CONSIDERED/IMPULSE/SUBSCRIPTION]
- Tone: [DESCRIBE]
- Can discount: [YES — max % / NO]
- Social proof: [REVIEWS, RATINGS, PRESS]

Think step by step about buyer psychology at 1hr, 24hr, and 48-72hr after abandonment.

**Email 1 (1 hour — Gentle Reminder):** Subject, preview text, body (helpful nudge, show product, link to cart), CTA "Complete your order."

**Email 2 (24 hours — Value + Social Proof):** Subject, preview, body (why it is worth it, review quote, address main objection), CTA.

**Email 3 (48-72 hours — Final Offer):** Subject, preview, body (scarcity or incentive — never lead with discount), CTA.

Include timing, open rate benchmarks, and suppression logic.
Notes: Custom GPT with your product catalog and review data enables personalized cart recovery at scale.

I need a 3-email abandoned cart recovery sequence.

**My store:**
- Business: [NAME] — [WHAT I SELL]
- Average cart: $[AMOUNT]
- Product type: [CONSIDERED/IMPULSE/SUBSCRIPTION]
- Tone: [DESCRIBE]
- Can I offer a discount: [YES — max % / NO]
- Social proof: [REVIEWS, RATINGS, PRESS]

Think through buyer psychology at each stage, then write:

**Email 1 (1 hour):** Gentle reminder — subject, preview, body, CTA. Helpful, not pushy.
**Email 2 (24 hours):** Value + social proof — subject, preview, body with review quote and objection handling, CTA.
**Email 3 (48-72 hours):** Urgency or incentive — subject, preview, body with scarcity, CTA.

Include timing and suppression notes.
Notes: Gemini can research best-performing cart recovery subject lines for your industry in real-time.

Unlock this prompt + all 74 in the library — free, updated monthly, zero spam.

Your email unlocks all 74 prompts instantly. No password, no signup — just your email. 

Help me write 3 emails to recover abandoned shopping carts.

**My store:**
- Business: [NAME] — [WHAT I SELL]
- Average cart value: $[AMOUNT]
- Type of purchase: [SOMETHING PEOPLE THINK ABOUT / IMPULSE BUY / SUBSCRIPTION]
- Brand tone: [DESCRIBE]
- Can I offer a discount: [YES — how much max / NO]
- Reviews or proof I can mention: [DESCRIBE]

Think about what the customer is feeling at each point, then write:

**Email 1 (send 1 hour after they leave):** Friendly reminder. Subject line, preview text, short email showing what they left behind, button to go back to their cart. Do not be pushy.

**Email 2 (send 24 hours later):** Show them why the product is great. Include a customer review or rating. Address the most common reason people hesitate. Button to go back.

**Email 3 (send 48-72 hours later):** Last chance. Create urgency — limited stock, cart expiring, or a small incentive if I can. Button to complete the purchase.

Tell me when to send each one and what to do if they buy after email 1 or 2.
Notes: Copilot in Outlook can help draft and schedule the sequence.
